_note: Structured from 'The Material Record of Scripture' catalog. Each artifact is evidence that can be folded into a chapter via the chapter's `evidence` list (by id).

tall-el-hammam.name: Tall el-Hammam destruction layer
tall-el-hammam.site: NE of the Dead Sea, Jordan Disk
tall-el-hammam.confirms: The fiery catastrophe of the Cities of the Plain (Sodom & Gomorrah), Genesis 19
tall-el-hammam.detail: A 1.5 m destruction layer showing extreme heat (>2000°C): bubbled pottery, melted mudbrick and shocked quartz, consistent with a cosmic airburst. A thriving city was wiped out instantly and the region lay empty for centuries.
tall-el-hammam.status: Debated — the 'airburst' Scientific Reports study was retracted/contested; the destruction itself is not in doubt.
tall-el-hammam.citation: Tall el-Hammam Excavation Project; cf. Genesis 19:24–28.

merneptah-stele.name: Merneptah Stele
merneptah-stele.confirms: Earliest extra-biblical mention of 'Israel' as a people in Canaan
merneptah-stele.detail: 'Israel is laid waste, his seed is not.' Israel carries the 'people' determinative (not 'city-state'), matching a tribal confederation in the Judges period.

soleb-yhw.name: Soleb Inscription ('Shasu of YHW')
soleb-yhw.confirms: Earliest occurrence of the divine name YHW outside the Bible
soleb-yhw.detail: Lists nomadic 'Shasu of YHW' in the regions south of Canaan — linking Yahweh-worship to the south centuries before the monarchy (cf. Deut 33:2).

jericho-cityiv.name: Jericho destruction (City IV)
jericho-cityiv.detail: Revetment walls collapsed outward forming a ramp; a thick burn layer; jars of charred grain left unplundered (short siege, city 'devoted' to destruction).
jericho-cityiv.citation: Kenyon & later excavations, Tell es-Sultan; cf. Joshua 6:20.

hazor-destruction.name: Hazor destruction & defaced idols
hazor-destruction.confirms: Joshua's burning of Hazor, 'head of all those kingdoms', Joshua 11:10
hazor-destruction.detail: The largest Canaanite city destroyed by fire intense enough to melt clay; statues of deities found systematically decapitated/defaced — pointing to iconoclastic (Israelite) conquerors.

mt-ebal-altar.name: Mount Ebal altar & lead curse tablet
mt-ebal-altar.confirms: Joshua's altar and the covenant ceremony, Joshua 8:30
mt-ebal-altar.detail: Rectangular altar approached by a ramp (not steps, per Exod 20:26), filled with bones of only kosher animals (no pig). A folded lead defixio allegedly reads 'Cursed… by the God YHW.'
mt-ebal-altar.status: Curse-tablet reading strongly disputed (Stripling/Galil vs. Rollston/Mazar).
mt-ebal-altar.citation: Zertal excavations; Associates for Biblical Research (2022).

deir-alla-balaam.name: Deir Alla (Balaam) Inscription
deir-alla-balaam.confirms: The non-Israelite prophet Balaam son of Beor, Numbers 22–24
deir-alla-balaam.detail: A plaster text titled 'The Book of Balaam son of Beor, the man who was a seer of the gods' — the same seer remembered in local Transjordan tradition.

tel-dan-stele.name: Tel Dan Stele ('House of David')
tel-dan-stele.confirms: The historical dynasty of David, the 'House of David' (Bytdwd)
tel-dan-stele.detail: An Aramean victory stele naming the king of 'the House of David' — extra-biblical proof of David's dynasty barely a century after his death.

khirbet-qeiyafa.name: Khirbet Qeiyafa (Elah fortress) & ostracon
khirbet-qeiyafa.confirms: A fortified Judahite state in David's reign; 'Shaaraim' (1 Sam 17:52)
khirbet-qeiyafa.detail: Carbon-dated olive pits fix the city to David's era; casemate wall with two gates ('Shaaraim'); a proto-Canaanite ostracon with melech (king), shofet (judge), eved (servant).

mesha-stele.name: Mesha Stele (Moabite Stone)
mesha-stele.confirms: Moab's revolt against Israel and Omri's oppression, 2 Kings 3
mesha-stele.detail: Longest Iron Age Levantine inscription. Names Omri king of Israel, the 'vessels of Yahweh', and (reconstructed, confirmed by 2023 imaging) the 'House of David'.
mesha-stele.status: Accepted; 'House of David' line confirmed by recent imaging.

kurkh-monolith.name: Kurkh Monolith (Battle of Qarqar)
kurkh-monolith.confirms: King Ahab of Israel as a major military power
kurkh-monolith.detail: Shalmaneser III's account of Qarqar names 'Ahab the Israelite' contributing 2,000 chariots — the largest chariot force in the anti-Assyrian coalition.

black-obelisk.name: Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III
black-obelisk.confirms: King Jehu of Israel — the only known image of an Israelite king
black-obelisk.detail: 'The tribute of Jehu, son of Omri' — a panel shows the king bowing before Shalmaneser III.

lachish-siege.name: Lachish Reliefs & siege ramp
lachish-siege.confirms: Sennacherib's siege of Lachish, 2 Kings 18; 2 Chron 32
lachish-siege.detail: Room-sized Nineveh palace reliefs depict the assault; the only known Assyrian siege ramp survives at the site, with a defenders' counter-ramp and a burnt destruction layer full of arrowheads and armor scales.
lachish-siege.citation: Lachish Reliefs, British Museum; Tel Lachish excavations.

sennacherib-prism.name: Taylor / Sennacherib Prism
sennacherib-prism.confirms: Sennacherib's failure to take Jerusalem from Hezekiah, 2 Kings 19
sennacherib-prism.detail: 'I shut up Hezekiah… like a caged bird in Jerusalem' — but conspicuously never claims to have captured the city, matching the biblical account of the Assyrian withdrawal.
sennacherib-prism.citation: Sennacherib Prism, British Museum / Oriental Institute.

hezekiah-tunnel.name: Siloam Tunnel Inscription & Broad Wall
hezekiah-tunnel.confirms: Hezekiah's water tunnel and city expansion, 2 Kings 20:20; Isa 22
hezekiah-tunnel.detail: A 533 m tunnel diverting the Gihon Spring, its inscription describing the two digging teams meeting; plus a 7 m-thick wall housing refugees from the fallen north.
hezekiah-tunnel.citation: Siloam Inscription, Istanbul Museum; Jewish Quarter excavations.

hezekiah-bulla.name: Hezekiah Bulla
hezekiah-bulla.confirms: King Hezekiah son of Ahaz of Judah
hezekiah-bulla.detail: Clay seal: 'Belonging to Hezekiah [son of] Ahaz king of Judah', with a winged sun-disk. Found 2015 in controlled excavation.

jeremiah-bullae.name: Jeremiah-era bullae (Gemariah, Jehucal, Gedaliah)
jeremiah-bullae.confirms: Named officials in the book of Jeremiah
jeremiah-bullae.detail: Clay seal impressions of Gemariah son of Shaphan (Jer 36:10), Jehucal son of Shelemiah (Jer 37:3) and Gedaliah son of Pashhur (Jer 38:1) — the last two found meters apart, in the 586 BCE destruction layer.

ketef-hinnom.name: Ketef Hinnom Silver Scrolls
ketef-hinnom.confirms: The Priestly Blessing, Numbers 6:24–26 — earliest biblical text known
ketef-hinnom.detail: Two tiny rolled silver scrolls bearing 'The LORD bless you and keep you…', predating the Dead Sea Scrolls by ~400 years and the pre-exilic existence of Torah liturgy.

lachish-letters.name: Lachish Letters (ostraca)
lachish-letters.detail: Urgent military dispatches: 'we are watching for the fire signals of Lachish… for we cannot see Azekah' — capturing the moment Azekah fell, leaving Lachish the last city before Jerusalem.
lachish-letters.citation: Lachish ostraca, British Museum / Israel Museum.

jehoiachin-tablets.name: Jehoiachin's Ration Tablets
jehoiachin-tablets.confirms: Exiled King Jehoiachin in Babylon, 2 Kings 25:27–30
jehoiachin-tablets.detail: Babylonian ration lists name 'Yaukin, king of the land of Yehud' and his five sons receiving oil — confirming the exiled king's treatment in captivity.

cyrus-cylinder.name: Cyrus Cylinder
cyrus-cylinder.confirms: Cyrus's policy of repatriation behind the return from exile, Ezra 1; 2 Chron 36
cyrus-cylinder.detail: Records Cyrus allowing deported peoples to return and rebuild their temples — the historical backdrop of the Jewish restoration (though it credits Marduk, not Yahweh).

pilate-stone.name: Pilate Stone
pilate-stone.confirms: Pontius Pilate, Prefect of Judea (the Gospels)
pilate-stone.detail: A dedication naming 'Pontius Pilatus, Prefect of Judea' — confirming both his existence and his exact Roman title (Praefectus).

caiaphas-ossuary.name: Caiaphas Ossuary
caiaphas-ossuary.confirms: The high priest Caiaphas of the Gospels
caiaphas-ossuary.detail: Inscribed 'Yehosef bar Qayafa' (Joseph son of Caiaphas), containing the bones of a ~60-year-old man — matching Josephus's 'Joseph who was called Caiaphas'.

pool-of-bethesda.name: Pool of Bethesda
pool-of-bethesda.detail: Excavation revealed a rectangular pool split by a central dyke — two basins, four surrounding colonnades plus a fifth on the dyke: exactly five porticoes, as John describes.

gallio-inscription.name: Gallio Inscription
gallio-inscription.confirms: Proconsul Gallio — fixes the date of Paul in Corinth, Acts 18:12
gallio-inscription.detail: Names 'Gallio, proconsul', allowing Paul's stay in Corinth to be dated precisely to 51–52 CE — an anchor point for all of NT chronology.

ain-ghazal-sixtoe.name: Ain Ghazal six-toed figurines
ain-ghazal-sixtoe.confirms: Polydactyly (six toes) modeled as a notable trait in the deep past
ain-ghazal-sixtoe.detail: Among the oldest large human statues ever made, some are deliberately modeled with six toes — the same hexadactyly the Bible records as a marker of the giant bloodline of Gath.

chaco-sixfinger.name: Chaco Canyon six-finger imagery
chaco-sixfinger.confirms: Six-fingered elite among the Ancestral Puebloans
chaco-sixfinger.detail: Rock art and burials at Chaco show six-fingered, six-toed individuals apparently of high status — the same polydactyly trait, in the American Southwest.

bashan-fortresses.name: Cyclopean fortresses of Bashan
bashan-fortresses.detail: 19th-century explorers documented massive basalt towns in Bashan — walls feet thick, stone doors 18 inches thick on stone hinges, roofs of giant slabs — structures on a scale awkward for ordinary humans, in the very region the Bible calls the land of giants.
bashan-fortresses.status: Structures real; 'built by giants' is the traditional claim.
bashan-fortresses.citation: J.L. Porter, The Giant Cities of Bashan (19th c.).

lovelock-finds.name: Lovelock Cave artifacts
lovelock-finds.confirms: The Paiute war on the Si-Te-Cah giants
lovelock-finds.detail: Excavations recovered ~10,000 artifacts including a 15-inch sandal and human bones with the marrow removed — physical echoes of the Paiute account of cannibal giants destroyed in the cave.
lovelock-finds.citation: L.L. Loud & M.R. Harrington, Lovelock Cave (1929).

dilmun-bahrain.name: Dilmun / Qal'at al-Bahrain
dilmun-bahrain.confirms: The Sumerian paradise land of Dilmun — the 'pure, clean, bright' garden-land where there is no sickness or old age
dilmun-bahrain.detail: Sumerian texts (Enki and Ninhursag; the Adapa Epic) describe Dilmun as the 'land of the living', a pure garden where lions don't kill, doves don't mourn, and there is no aging. Excavations at Qal'at al-Bahrain have confirmed a major Bronze Age city fed by abundant fresh-water springs, with extensive cuneiform trade records that fix its identity as the Dilmun of the texts.
dilmun-bahrain.status: Identification as Dilmun widely accepted among Assyriologists.
dilmun-bahrain.citation: Qal'at al-Bahrain UNESCO dossier; ETCSL 'Enki and Ninhursag' (the paradise of Dilmun).

apkallu-relief.name: Apkallu Fish-Sage Reliefs (Nimrud)
apkallu-relief.site: Nimrud (Kalhu), Assyria — British Museum and other collections
apkallu-relief.confirms: The Apkallu — the seven pre-flood divine sages who descended to teach civilization
apkallu-relief.detail: Large stone reliefs from the palace of Ashurnasirpal II at Nimrud depict two forms of Apkallu: eagle-headed winged figures and fish-cloaked beings carrying a ritual bucket and cone. These are the same seven sages Berossus (c. 280 BCE) lists as rising from the sea before the Flood to give humanity writing, law, architecture and every craft. The fish-cloaked figure is almost certainly the origin of the Oannes legend — a half-human, half-fish being who emerged from the waters to teach civilization.
apkallu-relief.status: Identification with the Apkallu tradition firmly accepted in Assyriology.
apkallu-relief.citation: Nimrud palace reliefs (Room I, panels), British Museum; cf. Berossus, Babyloniaca (on the Apkallu sages).

sumerian-king-list.name: Sumerian King List (Weld-Blundell Prism)
sumerian-king-list.site: Multiple findspots; best-preserved copy (Weld-Blundell Prism) at Ashmolean Museum, Oxford
sumerian-king-list.confirms: The Flood as a historical watershed dividing two eras — treated as chronological fact in Mesopotamia's own administrative record
sumerian-king-list.detail: Lists the kings of Sumer in sequence before and after 'the Flood swept over the earth.' Pre-flood kings reign for tens of thousands of years; post-flood kings have human-length reigns. The Flood is placed not in a mythological prologue but between named kings in a continuous administrative document — the ancient world's own equivalent of a dated chronicle. Roughly 18 copies are known.
sumerian-king-list.status: Accepted as a primary historical document; the long pre-flood reign-lengths are debated.
sumerian-king-list.citation: Sumerian King List; Thorkild Jacobsen ed. (Oriental Institute, 1939); ETCSL 2.1.1.

etemenanki-foundations.name: Etemenanki — excavated foundations (Babylon)
etemenanki-foundations.date: rebuilt c. 625–562 BCE under Nabopolassar and Nebuchadnezzar II
etemenanki-foundations.confirms: The literal tower built to 'rival heaven' — the historical basis for the Tower of Babel tradition
etemenanki-foundations.detail: The foundations of Etemenanki ('house of the foundation of heaven and earth') were excavated by Robert Koldewey (1899–1917). The square base measured 91.5 m per side; a Babylonian clay tablet (the Esagila Tablet) gives its planned height as equal to its base — approximately 90 m. Nebuchadnezzar II's own inscription reads: 'I raised the top of Etemenanki so that it rivalled heaven.' Herodotus visited c. 450 BCE and described eight ascending stages with a temple at the top.
etemenanki-foundations.status: Foundations firmly identified and measured; height reconstruction varies.
etemenanki-foundations.citation: Koldewey, Das wiedererstehende Babylon (1913); Esagila Tablet (AO 6555, Louvre); Herodotus, Histories I.181.

woolley-ur-flood.name: Woolley's Flood Layer — Ur
woolley-ur-flood.confirms: A catastrophic flood in southern Mesopotamia — a physical event behind the Flood traditions
woolley-ur-flood.detail: Leonard Woolley's excavations at Ur (1922–34) uncovered a clean silt deposit 2.4–3 m thick with no artifacts, separating two distinct occupation levels. Woolley identified it as 'the Flood.' Similar thick silt layers have been found at Shuruppak, Kish and other Mesopotamian cities in roughly the same period, pointing to historically real, catastrophically large flooding in the very homeland of the written Flood tradition — including the city whose name (Shuruppak) appears in the Sumerian King List as the last city before the Flood.
woolley-ur-flood.status: Flood layers physically confirmed; debate concerns whether they represent one event or multiple local floods.
woolley-ur-flood.citation: Woolley, Ur Excavations vol. II: The Royal Cemetery (1934); cf. Mallowan, Iraq 26 (1964) on Mesopotamian flood deposits.

gilgamesh-flood-tablet.name: The Flood Tablet (Gilgamesh XI, K.3375)
gilgamesh-flood-tablet.confirms: The full flood narrative — warning, vessel, animals, birds, mountain, sacrifice — circulating in writing centuries before the oldest surviving biblical manuscripts
gilgamesh-flood-tablet.detail: The most famous cuneiform tablet in the world. In 1872 George Smith, a self-taught British Museum assistant sorting tablet fragments, suddenly read the lines about a ship on a mountain and a dove sent out — and reportedly began tearing off his clothes in excitement. Its publication stunned Victorian England: the flood account of Genesis, on clay, from the library of an Assyrian king, in a version whose story-line was already a thousand years old when the tablet was copied.
gilgamesh-flood-tablet.status: Firmly accepted; on permanent display in the British Museum.
gilgamesh-flood-tablet.citation: Tablet K.3375, British Museum; George Smith, 'The Chaldean Account of the Deluge' (1872).

ark-tablet.name: The Ark Tablet ('two by two')
ark-tablet.site: unrecorded findspot; studied and published at the British Museum
ark-tablet.confirms: The animals boarding the ark 'two by two' — in Babylonian, a millennium before the oldest surviving Genesis manuscripts
ark-tablet.detail: A 60-line builder's specification in which the god Enki tells Atra-hasis exactly how to build the life-boat: a giant round coracle of rope and wood, an area of one field, waterproofed inside and out with two kinds of bitumen — and the wild animals entering it 'two by two' (šana). Translated by Irving Finkel ('The Ark Before Noah,' 2014). A 35-tonne replica built to the tablet's specifications in 2014 actually floated.
ark-tablet.status: Tablet and reading accepted; as a privately held, unprovenanced object its findspot is unknown — noted honestly.
ark-tablet.citation: The Ark Tablet; I. Finkel, 'The Ark Before Noah: Decoding the Story of the Flood' (2014).
